rationale: The page concludes the section on tribal education initiatives (specifically digital platforms and scholarships) and introduces the Juvenile Justice System in India. It details the legal framework (JJ Act 2015), objectives, definitions, and key institutional mechanisms like Juvenile Justice Boards and Child Welfare Committees.

Other Education Initiatives for Tribals:
• Pre-Matric Scholarships to ST students and Post Matric Scholarship to ST students: Pre – Matric is applicable to students who are studying in Classes IX –X. Post metric is to provide financial assistance to Scheduled Tribe students studying at post-matriculation or post-secondary levels. For Both Scholarshipso Parental income from all sources should not be more than Rs.2.50 lakhs per annum.
o Funding ratio is 75:25 between Centre and States for all States except North East and Hills States/UT like Himachal Pradesh, Uttarakhand and Jammu and Kashmir where it is 90:10. For UTs without legislature sharing pattern is 100% Central share.
8.8. JUVENILE JUSTICE SYSTEM IN INDIA
Why in the News?
The recently released report “Juvenile Justice and Children in Conflict with the Law: A Study of Capacity at the Frontlines” by the India Justice Report.
More on the News
• The report evaluated the functioning of the Indian Juvenile Justice System ten years after enactment of Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act 2015 (JJ Act, 2015).
• Led by Tata Trusts, in collaboration with civil society organizations, The India Justice Report provides data-driven insights to track improvements and deficits in the formal justice system.
System of Juvenile Justice in India
• Legal Basis: Governed by the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015, which replaced the JJ Act, 2000; amended by the JJ (Care and Protection of Children) Amendment Act, 2021.
• Objective: Consolidates laws relating to children in conflict with law and children in need of care and protection, focusing on care, rehabilitation, and social reintegration.
• Definitions:
o Child: Person who has not completed 18 years of age.
o Juvenile: A child below 18 years.
o Child in conflict with law: A child below 18 years alleged or found to have committed an offence.
• Key Provisions of JJ Act, 2015
o Juvenile Justice Boards (JJBs): Established in every district to adjudicate cases of children in conflict with law.
o Monitoring Mechanism:
National level – National Commission for Protection of Child Rights (NCPCR).
State level – State Commissions for Protection of Child Rights (SCPCR).
o Preliminary Assessment for Heinous Offences:
Applicable when a child aged 16–18 years is alleged to have committed a heinous offence.
JJB assesses mental and physical capacity, ability to understand consequences, and circumstances of offence.
Children’s Court decides whether the child should be tried as an adult.
o Child Welfare Committees (CWCs):
Constituted in every district by State Governments.
